summary: Julia Evans shares her experience switching from Vim to the Helix text editor after 20 years of Vim usage. The primary motivation was getting language server support without extensive configuration. She finds Helix's built-in LSP support, search with full context, and inline keybinding help significantly better than her Vim setup. While she notes several annoyances including poor Markdown list handling and occasional crashes, the switch was easier than expected. Her Helix configuration is remarkably minimal compared to her hundreds-of-lines Neovim config.
key_points: Built-in language server support was the killer feature that motivated the switch, eliminating the configuration burden that plagued both custom and pre-built Vim/Neovim setups
Multiple cursors replaced macros as her primary batch-editing workflow, and she finds them preferable
Relearning 20 years of muscle memory was easier than expected, especially by embracing the 'Helix way' rather than trying to force Vim keybindings
The configuration went from hundreds of lines in Neovim to essentially 4 keyboard shortcuts
Several annoyances remain, particularly around Markdown editing (list continuation, text reflowing), but none severe enough to drive her back to Vim
money_quotes: After using Vim/Neovim for 20 years, I've tried both "build my own custom configuration from scratch" and "use someone else's pre-buld configuration system" and even though I love Vim I was excited about having things just work without having to work on my configuration at all.
The first time I tried Helix I tried to force it to use keybindings that were more similar to Vim and that did not work for me. Just learning the "Helix way" was a lot easier.
I appreciate that my configuration is really simple, compared to my neovim configuration which is hundreds of lines. It's mostly just 4 keyboard shortcuts.
I like multiple cursors a lot more than writing macros all the time.
I was worried that relearning 20 years of Vim muscle memory would be really hard. It turned out to be easier than I expected.
The "markdown list" and reflowing issues come up a lot for me because I spend a lot of time editing Markdown lists, but I keep using Helix anyway so I guess they can't be making me that mad.
key_insight: The best tool configuration is the one you don't have to maintain — built-in sensible defaults can outweigh decades of customized muscle memory.
